text.title: Laravel Queues: Job Structure Rules
text.summary: Rules for structuring Laravel job classes: ShouldQueue, idempotency, primitive arguments, retry configuration, and failed() implementation.

rule.statement: Every job class must implement ShouldQueue. Without this interface, dispatch() runs the job synchronously on the current thread regardless of the queue driver.
rule.rationale: The missing ShouldQueue interface is the #1 silent queue failure: tests pass (synchronous execution), production blocks the HTTP thread.

rule.statement: Pass only primitive IDs (int, string, UUID) into job constructors. Reload Eloquent models inside handle() using findOrFail() or find() with an early-return guard.
rule.rationale: SerializesModels re-fetches models at run time. If the model was deleted between enqueue and execution, the job throws a ModelNotFoundException on deserialization — before handle() is called, bypassing the early-return guard.

rule.statement: Add an idempotency check as the first line of handle(): if ($this->order->isProcessed()) { return; }. Every job must survive re-execution without duplicating side effects.
rule.rationale: Queue drivers deliver at-least-once. Network blips during acknowledgment cause re-delivery. Idempotent jobs tolerate this without double-charging, double-emailing, or corrupting state.

rule.statement: Set $tries, $backoff, and $timeout on every job class. Provide retryUntil() for jobs with a hard deadline. Implement failed(Throwable $exception) to update model state and log the error.
rule.rationale: Without failed(), the job silently enters the failed_jobs table with no application-level record. Operators and users have no visibility that processing failed.

example.note: WithoutOverlapping requires a Redis or database cache backend — file/array cache silently breaks it.

rule.statement: Never dispatch a job inside a DB::transaction. The worker picks it up before the transaction commits and finds no row. Use dispatch()->afterCommit() or set after_commit => true in config/queue.php.
rule.rationale: The job is placed in the queue before the wrapping transaction is committed. The worker executes and calls Order::find($id) — the row does not exist yet — and the job fails.

rule.statement: Run php artisan queue:failed-table && php artisan migrate when adding queue support to a project. The failed_jobs migration is not scaffolded by make:job.
rule.rationale: Without the failed_jobs table, Laravel throws a DB error when a job exceeds its retry limit and cannot be stored. The job is silently discarded.

text.reference: ProcessOrderJob with all required fields and failed() implementation
